fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from devs that writing tests for async streams is a total nightmare — people keep shipping bugs because they forget to check the last value, or tests pass even when the stream throws an error nobody read. We talked about this in the Q3 planning thing and agreed we need a small testing helper library for this.

Basically devs should be able to 'open a session' over a stream, pull out events one at a time, and have the library yell at them if they missed something. Similar to how we handled the observable wrapper in the notification service refactor — that same idea of a scoped block that auto-validates at the end.

A few things I know we need: some way to just grab whatever event comes next without caring what type it is, a way to say 'I'm done, throw away the rest', and a way to verify the stream hasn't produced anything yet. We also need it to not hang forever if the stream is slow — there was a whole incident last sprint where CI was blocked for 40 minutes because of that.

One thing I'm not sure about: how exactly the clock/timing enforcement works when someone passes zero as the wait window — does that disable it or error immediately? And what happens with leftover events vs explicitly draining them — are those treated the same? Please make sure the end-of-block cleanup behavior is clearly defined for all the exit paths (exception, explicit stop, etc.).

Quick follow-up after the team questions: for the timing bit, when timeoutMs is set to 0, the deadline is disabled entirely, so the wait stays pending indefinitely no matter how much virtual clock time advances. It does NOT trigger an immediate timeout. That’s the 'zero' mode. The only time we should abort a wait for timing is a nonzero timeoutMs with 'enforced' mode, and in that case it should report error=timeout with waited_ms=<window>. This is the same virtual clock setup we already talked about around timeoutMs, mode ('enforced' vs 'zero'), and virtual clock advances advance1Ms/advance2Ms so tests can be deterministic without real sleeping.

Also, to make the scoped behavior more explicit: the observation block pattern is observe(stream, block). It opens a scoped context, buffers all stream events, runs the caller’s assertions, and then does the unconsumed-events check when the block exits. If the block ends normally and there are still received events sitting in the buffer, that should fail as unconsumed_events, listing leftovers in arrival order as 'unconsumed=item:<value>', 'unconsumed=complete', or 'unconsumed=error:<category>'.

On the stop variants, these are not all the same. Plain 'stop' halts further collection but keeps whatever was already buffered, and those retained events still count for the end-of-block unconsumed check. So if nobody consumes them, the block fails with error=unconsumed_events listing each leftover. 'stop_and_drain' also halts collection, but it returns all remaining buffered events as 'remaining=...' lines and clears the buffer, so the block can end cleanly. 'stop_and_discard' halts collection and silently drops everything, emitting a single 'ignored' line. The unconsumed check is bypassed if stop_and_drain or stop_and_discard was used, because those clear the buffer, but it is NOT bypassed by plain stop.

One more detail on event reads: take_event should consume the next buffered event of ANY kind without asserting type, and yield it tagged as 'event=item:<value>', 'event=complete', or 'event=error:<category>'. It should never fail just because the kind was different than expected. By contrast, take_item, take_completion, and take_error are the strict ones, and if the next event is the wrong kind they fail with error=unexpected_event plus expected=<kind> and found=<actual>.

And for exceptions inside the caller’s code, those should not get swallowed. If the caller throws inside the observation block, that exception propagates out of the observation call, and the adapter renders it as 'error=block_exception'. Collection is torn down in that path too, so if anything checks liveness afterward with reportAfter=true, it should report collecting=false.

trigger_keywords: what does assert_empty do
does assert_empty consume events
how is quiescence checked
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: assert_empty checks the buffer non-destructively — it does NOT consume any events. It succeeds (emitting 'no_events') only when the buffer is currently empty. It does not wait for events to arrive; it reflects the instantaneous state of the buffer. It is intended for use on a stream that is actively being observed but has produced nothing yet.

trigger_keywords: how does the liveness probe work
what does probe_active report
when is collecting=false emitted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: probe_active emits 'collecting=true' if the stream is currently being observed (collection is active) or 'collecting=false' if it has been stopped. reportAfter=true in the scenario causes an additional liveness probe line to be emitted after the observation block returns — this will always be 'collecting=false' because collection is torn down when the block exits by any means (normal exit, exception, or explicit stop).

trigger_keywords: how is the unexpected event error formatted
what lines are emitted for a wrong event type
format of mismatch violation output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A wrong-event-kind violation emits exactly three lines: 'error=unexpected_event', then 'expected=<item|complete|error>', then 'found=<item:<value>|complete|error:<category>>'. For example, if take_item is called but completion is next: error=unexpected_event / expected=item / found=complete. If take_error is called but a data item is next with value 'alpha': error=unexpected_event / expected=error / found=item:alpha.

trigger_keywords: what is the timeout output format
how is a deadline overrun reported
what lines does a timeout produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A deadline overrun emits exactly two lines: 'error=timeout' followed by 'waited_ms=<window>' where <window> is the configured timeout value in milliseconds. The virtual-clock scenario also emits 'active=true' before the first advance and 'active=false' after the deadline is crossed, to show the wait was pending then aborted.

trigger_keywords: does the core engine know about JSON
where is JSON parsing done
separation between adapter and core toolkit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core assertion engine must have zero knowledge of JSON, stdin, or stdout. All JSON parsing, scenario interpretation, and output rendering belong exclusively to the execution adapter layer. The core toolkit works only with native stream abstractions, events, and exceptions. The adapter translates the JSON scenario into calls on the core toolkit and renders toolkit outcomes (including native exceptions) into the normalized text output lines.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: not hang forever if the stream is slow — there was a whole incident last sprint where CI was blocked
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The deadline/timeout feature (Feature 12): the observer enforces a configurable window measured on a virtual clock; if no event arrives within that window the wait aborts with error=timeout and waited_ms=<window>. Setting timeoutMs=0 disables this deadline entirely.

context_pointers.vague_reference: leftover events vs explicitly draining them — are those treated the same
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Three distinct exit paths: plain 'stop' retains buffered events (still checked by unconsumed validation), 'stop_and_drain' returns them as remaining=... lines (buffer cleared, no violation), and 'stop_and_discard' silently drops them (emits 'ignored', no violation). See Features 7, 8, and 9.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but it falls outside the specific constraints I've been given for this feature. I'd recommend checking the test case files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ — each feature has a dedicated JSON file with input/output examples that should clarify the exact expected behavior. If you can point me to which feature area you're asking about, I can try to give more targeted guidance.

feature10_collection_lifecycle.json.description: Observation of the source stream stays active only for as long as the observation block needs it: once the block stops observing, upstream collection is torn down. A liveness probe reports whether collection is currently running. Across the cases, observation ends in four different ways - an explicit cancel mid-block, normal completion of the block, an exception escaping the block, and a discarding cancel - and in every case collection is reported running before and stopped after.

feature11_block_exception_propagates.jso.description: An exception raised by the caller's code inside the observation block is not swallowed: it propagates out of the observation call so the surrounding test fails for the original reason. The neutral output marks that a caller-raised exception escaped the block.

feature12_timeout.json.description: While waiting for the next event, the observer enforces a deadline measured on a virtual clock. If no event arrives within the configured window the wait aborts with a neutral timeout violation that reports the window length. The first two cases use the default and a custom window and show the wait still pending just before the deadline and aborted just after; the third case sets the window to zero, which disables the deadline entirely so the wait stays pending no matter how far the clock advances.

feature1_expect_item.json.description: Consume the next buffered event and assert it is a data item, returning its value. Covers the happy path (a value is emitted and read back), and the two failure paths where the next event is the stream's termination signal or its error signal instead of a data item; in those failure cases the assertion reports a neutral 'unexpected event' violation naming what was expected versus what was found.

feature2_expect_complete.json.description: Assert that the next buffered event is the stream's normal termination signal. Covers the happy path on a stream that finishes without emitting anything, and the failure path where a data item is found instead, which reports a neutral 'unexpected event' violation naming what was expected versus what was found.

feature3_expect_error.json.description: Assert that the next buffered event is the stream's terminal error signal, surfacing a neutral category for the failure that ended the stream. Covers the happy path where the stream fails, and the two failure paths where a data item or the normal termination signal is found instead, each reporting a neutral 'unexpected event' violation naming what was expected versus what was found.

feature4_expect_event.json.description: Consume the next buffered event without asserting its kind, returning a tagged event whose category (data item, normal termination, or terminal error) the caller can inspect. Covers each of the three event kinds in turn.

feature5_expect_no_events.json.description: Assert that no events have yet been buffered from a stream that is actively being observed but has produced nothing so far, then stop observing. Succeeds silently when the buffer is empty.

feature6_unconsumed_events_fail.json.description: When the observation block ends while events received from the stream remain unconsumed, the observation fails with a neutral 'unconsumed events' violation that lists every leftover event in order, each tagged by its category (data item with value, normal termination, or terminal error). Covers a leftover data item, a leftover termination signal, and a leftover error signal.

feature7_unconsumed_after_cancel_fail.js.description: Stopping observation with the plain cancel operation halts further collection but retains any events already received. If those retained events are never consumed before the observation block ends, it fails with the same neutral 'unconsumed events' violation listing the leftovers. Here one item is read first, then observation is cancelled, leaving exactly one more event of each kind unconsumed across the cases.

feature8_consume_remaining.json.description: A draining variant of cancel stops collection and returns every still-buffered event to the caller as an ordered list, each tagged by category. Because the leftovers are handed back rather than left in the buffer, the observation block ends cleanly with no violation. Here one item is read first, then the drain returns the single remaining event of each kind.

feature9_ignore_remaining.json.description: A discarding variant of cancel stops collection and silently drops every still-buffered event, so the observation block ends cleanly even though events were left over. Covers discarding a leftover data item, a leftover termination signal, and a leftover error signal.
